title: Ruptured Ectopic Pregnancy
chief_complaint: I have severe sharp pain in my right lower abdomen. I feel dizzy and like I'm going to faint. I missed my period 6 weeks ago and the pregnancy test was positive. Now I have some dark spotting.

physical_exam.Abdomen: Peritoneal signs: Rebound tenderness, guarding. Distended. Shifting dullness (hemoperitoneum).
physical_exam.Pelvic: Cervical motion tenderness (exquisite). Right adnexal mass (4cm, tender). Uterus small, non-tender. Dark blood in vaginal vault.
physical_exam.General: Pale, diaphoretic. Class III hemorrhagic shock (cold extremities, thready pulse, delayed capillary refill).

hidden_data.bedside_us: Empty uterus (no intrauterine pregnancy). Right adnexal mass with fetal pole and cardiac activity (ectopic pregnancy). Large amount of free fluid in pelvis and abdomen (hemoperitoneum).
hidden_data.culdocentesis: Culdocentesis: Non-clotting blood aspirated (positive for hemoperitoneum)

correct_diagnosis: Ruptured Ectopic Pregnancy (Right Tubal) with Hemoperitoneum and Hemorrhagic Shock - SURGICAL EMERGENCY
key_learning_points: Ectopic pregnancy = implantation outside uterine cavity. 95% tubal (ampulla 70%, isthmus 12%, fimbria 11%). Leading cause of maternal death in first trimester (10% of pregnancy-related deaths)
Risk factors: Prior ectopic (15% recurrence), PID/tubal damage (most common), Prior tubal surgery, IUD, Smoking, Age >35, IVF. However, 50% have NO risk factors

Presentation: Classic triad (only 50%): Amenorrhea + Abdominal pain + Vaginal bleeding. Shoulder pain (diaphragmatic irritation from hemoperitoneum). Syncope/shock = ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise

Diagnosis: Beta-hCG + Transvaginal ultrasound. Discriminatory zone: Beta-hCG 1,500-2,000 = should see IUP on TVUS. If no IUP + hCG above discriminatory zone = ectopic. Empty uterus + adnexal mass + free fluid = ectopic. Fetal cardiac activity outside uterus = diagnostic

Management: HEMODYNAMICALLY UNSTABLE: EMERGENCY LAPAROTOMY (not laparoscopy - faster). Salpingectomy (remove tube) if contralateral tube normal. Salpingostomy (preserve tube) if contralateral tube damaged/absent and desires future fertility. Rhogam if Rh-negative. HEMODYNAMICALLY STABLE: Medical (Methotrexate 50mg/m² IM single dose) if: Unruptured, Mass <3.5cm, No cardiac activity, hCG <5,000, Reliable follow-up. Surgical: Laparoscopic salpingectomy or salpingostomy. Expectant: hCG <200 and declining

Post-op: Serial beta-hCG until zero (weekly). Persistent trophoblastic tissue (5-15% after salpingostomy). Methotrexate if hCG plateauing or rising. Contraception: Discuss future fertility. Recurrence risk 15%. Early ultrasound in next pregnancy (location scan at 6-7 weeks)
